label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's look at these two statements to see which one is sarcastic. First, let's recall what sarcasm is. Sarcasm is a form of irony where someone says the opposite of what they actually mean, often in a sharp or mocking way. It's usually used to convey the opposite of one's real thoughts or feelings and may include irony or subtle ridicule. Let's examine option (A): "Boy, I sure love it when bigots show up in a thread and start getting nosy." - The speaker is saying "I sure love it" (which is a positive expression) when bigots show up and start getting nosy (which is a negative situation). - This seems to be straightforward sarcasm because the speaker is expressing the opposite of their true feelings. They probably don't actually love it when bigots show up and be nosy, but they're saying they do in a sarcastic way. Let's examine option (B): "Boy, I sure love it when bigots show up in a thread and start getting roasted." - Again, the speaker is using "I sure love it" (a positive expression) about bigots showing up. - However, the action is "getting roasted," which means being criticized or mocked in a humorous manner. - In this case, the speaker might be expressing genuine enjoyment when bigots are exposed to criticism, so it might not be sarcasm. The speaker might actually mean they love seeing bigots get mocked. Comparing the two, option (A) is more clearly sarcastic because the speaker expresses a positive sentiment about a negative situation
